topic: IA & interaction-design patterns for multi-pane creative/IDE web apps; applied to a 6-workspace fiction-writing + world-simulation tool

cards.title: How we redesigned the Linear UI (part II)
cards.key_claim: Linear's 2024 refresh dimmed the sidebar so the main content takes precedence, made tabs more compact (rounded, smaller icons/text) instead of full-width, and used view headers + side panels to surface filters/meta properties without crowding the canvas.
cards.relevance: Direct template for our left rail + content + right meta panel; shows how to reduce visual weight of nav so writing canvas dominates.

cards.title: Linear Changelog — Collapsible Sidebar
cards.key_claim: Sidebar can collapse to give the canvas full width on demand; navigation becomes a peek/hover surface.
cards.relevance: Justifies a togglable rail (Cmd+\) for our Writing workspace where prose density matters most.

cards.title: Linear Concepts (Workspace, Teams, Views)
cards.key_claim: A Linear workspace is a single container; views are dynamic filtered slices of one underlying graph rather than separate apps.
cards.relevance: Maps onto our problem: World/Memory/Atlas should be views over one underlying entity graph, not three separate workspaces.

cards.title: Linear — Design for the AI age
cards.key_claim: AI in Linear is summoned via command palette / inline context, not pinned chrome; the product avoids 'AI everywhere' chrome drift.
cards.relevance: Counterpoint for the 'AI everywhere' anti-pattern; supports putting Simulation/Runtime AI behind palette + inline triggers, not always-visible panels.

cards.title: Linear design: the SaaS design trend that's boring and bettering UI
cards.key_claim: Linear's 2025 monochrome palette and reduced color have become a copied baseline; saturated color is reserved for state, not chrome.
cards.relevance: Argues for our chrome being neutral/monochrome and reserving saturated colors for in-prose semantics (sim events, world entities).

cards.title: VS Code — User Interface
cards.key_claim: VS Code's six regions: Activity Bar, Primary Side Bar, Editor Group(s), Panel, Secondary Side Bar, Status Bar. Activity Bar is the persistent mode switch; sidebars host views; editor groups own tabs.
cards.relevance: Canonical 'verbs (activity bar) + nouns (sidebar views) + content (editor)' decomposition we should adopt for our 6 workspaces.

cards.title: VS Code — Custom Layout
cards.key_claim: Users can drag/move views between Primary/Secondary sidebar and Panel regions; layout is remembered per workbench.
cards.relevance: Supports moveable, persistable panels rather than hard-coded conditional rendering.

cards.title: VS Code Extension API — UX Guidelines: Sidebars
cards.key_claim: Primary side bar = navigation/structure; Secondary side bar = ancillary opposite-side context; both can host arbitrary views.
cards.relevance: Two-rail asymmetric layout (structure left, context right) is the field-tested pattern for IDE-style workbenches.

cards.title: Cursor Forum — Flexible Panel Layout request
cards.key_claim: Cursor's fixed three-pane (sidebar / editor / AI chat) layout draws repeated user complaints; users want movable AI panel position.
cards.relevance: Cautionary tale: even successful AI-IDE products get pushback when the right-hand AI rail is locked. Bake in panel mobility.

cards.title: Cursor Forum — Customizable AI Panel Layout Position
cards.key_claim: Users explicitly ask for the AI panel to be movable / dockable; rigid right-only AI panels feel cramped on small screens.
cards.relevance: For our Simulation + Runtime panels, allow docking left/right/bottom and a 'pop out' Composition Mode.

cards.title: Notion — New sidebar design will help Notion scale
cards.key_claim: Notion split sidebar into Workspace / Teamspaces / Private sections; teamspaces act as the broadest categorization, hierarchically nesting pages below.
cards.relevance: Supports a hierarchical 'project > book > chapter > scene' sidebar over flat tab switching for Writing workspace.

cards.title: UI Breakdown of Notion's Sidebar (Medium)
cards.key_claim: Notion sidebar is 224px wide on an 8px grid; uses accordion drill-down; right-side line nav doubles as in-page TOC.
cards.relevance: Concrete grid + width numbers for our left rail; argues for adding an in-doc outline to the writing canvas.

cards.title: Design Critique: Notion — Autonomy, Cleanness, Organization
cards.key_claim: Notion's effectiveness comes from progressive disclosure: blocks, slash commands, and toggle headings hide complexity until summoned.
cards.relevance: Justifies / commands inside the writing surface to expose Simulation/World features without permanent chrome.

cards.title: Obsidian Graph view documentation
cards.key_claim: Obsidian distinguishes global graph (whole vault) from local graph (current note's neighborhood), each with filter sidebar.
cards.relevance: Direct precedent for our Atlas: we should ship a 'local atlas' (entities mentioned in current scene) before a 'global atlas'.

cards.title: Obsidian Forum — Open local graph in right sidebar
cards.key_claim: Power users mount the local graph as a right-sidebar pane next to their note, so the graph stays in peripheral vision while writing.
cards.relevance: Pattern for keeping Atlas/World context visible without taking the writing screen real estate.

cards.title: Figma — Design, prototype, and explore layer properties in the right sidebar
cards.key_claim: Right sidebar is contextual: Design / Prototype / Inspect. Content swaps based on selection. Empty selection collapses the panel.
cards.relevance: Inspector pattern: panel content should be contextual to selection, and should self-collapse when nothing is selected (preventing 'database wall' problem).

cards.title: Figma — View layers and pages in the left sidebar
cards.key_claim: Left sidebar = structure (pages, layers). Two-tab toggle (Layers / Assets). Mirrors the IDE pattern of structure-left.
cards.relevance: Reinforces that structure-left / context-right is the dominant creative-tool pattern, not 6 sibling top-level workspaces.

cards.title: Figma Forum — Option to hide the right panel
cards.key_claim: Even Figma users repeatedly ask to fully hide the right panel for canvas focus; current behavior auto-collapses but minimum width still bothers users.
cards.relevance: Confirms inspectors must be hidable, not merely 'minimize-able', for focus-mode work.

cards.title: Sudowrite vs Novelcrafter — Canvas overview
cards.key_claim: Sudowrite Canvas is a freeform card board for character ideas / plot / prose; supports Hero's Journey / Save the Cat / 3-Act / Romancing the Beat overlays. Novelcrafter is panel-dense (manuscript + Codex side-by-side).
cards.relevance: Two valid templates for fiction tools: Sudowrite = uncluttered + AI-summon, Novelcrafter = context-at-a-glance dense panels. Our app currently leans Novelcrafter-dense without the discipline; we should pick a stance.

cards.title: Novelcrafter — Your Novel Writing Software
cards.key_claim: Novelcrafter explicitly markets a 'Codex' (worldbuilding entities) panel that travels with the manuscript; structure-left, manuscript-center, codex-right.
cards.relevance: Strongest direct analog to our World/Memory functions. Confirms structure/manuscript/codex three-pane is a workable IA for fiction with worldbuilding.

cards.title: Literature & Latte — Write Without Distractions Using Composition Mode
cards.key_claim: Composition Mode hides chrome; only a thin Control Bar appears on cursor proximity. Typewriter Scrolling pins the active line vertically centered.
cards.relevance: Template for a 'Writing Focus' mode toggled with one shortcut, removing all 6 workspaces from view temporarily.

cards.title: Skyler Benson — How to Use Scrivener Typewriter Mode
cards.key_claim: Typewriter Scrolling reduces the head-tilt fatigue of writing toward the bottom of the screen by keeping the cursor mid-screen.
cards.relevance: Concrete distraction-reduction interaction we can implement in our Writing surface independent of mode toggles.

cards.title: Kindlepreneur — Scrivener vs Ulysses
cards.key_claim: Ulysses ships dark mode + minimal mode + full-screen + typewriter mode as toggleable single-purpose focus tools.
cards.relevance: Argues we should not bundle everything in one 'focus mode' but offer composable focus toggles.

cards.title: NN/G — 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design
cards.key_claim: Updated Jan 2024. Heuristic 6 (Recognition rather than recall): show options instead of forcing memory. Heuristic 8 (Aesthetic and minimalist): every extra element competes with relevant content. Heuristic 9 (Help users recover from errors): clear, plain-language error states.
cards.relevance: Direct grounding for command palette (recall→recognition), reducing chrome in 1659-line shell (minimalist), and replacing JSON dumps with friendly errors.

cards.title: NN/G — 10 Heuristics Applied to Complex Applications
cards.key_claim: Complex tools (IDE-class) should use progressive disclosure plus consistent meta-pattern (left structure, center work, right context) to keep heuristics intact under feature load.
cards.relevance: Most direct guidance for our 6-workspace problem; supports collapsing 6 into a stable 3-region workbench.

cards.title: NN/G — Progressive Disclosure
cards.key_claim: Defer secondary options to a subsidiary screen; show primary options by default. Improves task-completion speed 30-50% while preserving feature discoverability ~70-90%.
cards.relevance: Foundational source for hiding Simulation/Runtime advanced controls behind 'Advanced' disclosure / palette commands.

cards.title: NN/G — Information Foraging: a theory of how people navigate the web
cards.key_claim: Users follow 'information scent': trigger words in labels, headings, link text. Specific labels reduce navigation time 30-50% vs generic.
cards.relevance: Renaming the 6 generic workspace names ('World', 'Memory', 'Atlas') to action/content labels (e.g. 'Codex', 'Lore', 'Map') strengthens scent.

cards.title: UX Tigers — Information Scent: How Users Decide Where to Click
cards.key_claim: Trigger words in nav labels make-or-break clickthrough; vague labels push users to search.
cards.relevance: If our left rail says 'World' and 'Memory' and 'Atlas', users can't predict the difference. Concrete fix candidate.

cards.title: NN/G — Scrolling and Attention
cards.key_claim: 57% of viewing time is spent above the fold; 74% in first two screenfuls. First impression formed in <0.05s.
cards.relevance: Argues that whatever we put in the first viewport of the Writing workspace IS the product perception. Currently competing toolbars hurt this.

cards.title: Butterick's Practical Typography — Line Length
cards.key_claim: Average line length should be 45–90 characters including spaces.
cards.relevance: Sets the max-width on the prose column in our Writing workspace; 65ch is a strong default.

cards.title: Butterick's Practical Typography — Summary of Key Rules
cards.key_claim: Body text: 10–12pt print / 15–25px web. Line spacing 120–145% of size. Avoid system defaults (Times/Arial).
cards.relevance: Concrete numeric grounding for our prose CSS.

cards.title: Typotheque — Typesetting principles of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean (CJK) text
cards.key_claim: CJK characters fill the em-box; ~170% line-height is needed to avoid cramped strokes. CJK kerning between punctuation improves readability.
cards.relevance: Critical for Bagua / 八卦 fiction app: leading should be 1.7 for prose body, not 1.4-1.5 default.

cards.title: Asian Absolute — CJK Typesetting in 2025
cards.key_claim: WCAG limit for CJK text is 40 characters per line vs 80 for Latin; 80 Chinese characters becomes 'too dense and exhausting'.
cards.relevance: Our prose column max-width must be expressed in CJK character count (~36-40ch CJK), not 65ch Latin.

cards.title: AZ-Loc — Best Chinese Fonts for Websites (2026)
cards.key_claim: Source Han Serif / Noto Serif CJK SC for prose body; Source Han Sans / PingFang / Misans for UI chrome. Differentiating prose vs UI font is essential.
cards.relevance: Directly informs our typography stack: serif for manuscript text, sans for UI chrome.

cards.title: UXPin — Optimal Line Length for Readability (2026)
cards.key_claim: Latin: 50–75 chars/line ideal, 45–90 acceptable; CJK shorter; lines longer than 90 hurt eye-tracking returns.
cards.relevance: Confirms our hard cap should be 90ch even in low-density mode.

cards.title: iA — feed (Web design is 95% typography / 100E2R)
cards.key_claim: 100E2R = 100% Easy to Read. Body fonts 16px+; macro-typography (line length, leading) matters more than font choice.
cards.relevance: Mentor-source for prose-first IA: typography drives the writing experience, not chrome.

cards.title: SAP Fiori — Content Density (Cozy and Compact)
cards.key_claim: Cozy = touch / casual; Compact = power-user / data-dense. Provide a user toggle, never auto-pick.
cards.relevance: Argue for a Comfortable / Compact toggle so reviewers/editors can switch from prose-comfort to outline-density.

cards.title: Cloudscape Design System — Content Density
cards.key_claim: Compact density not fully applied to read-oriented surfaces because it hurts reading; long-form reading should default to comfortable density.
cards.relevance: Default state for our Writing workspace = comfortable; Atlas/Memory tables can lean compact.

cards.title: Matt Ström-Awn — UI Density
cards.key_claim: Density is about information per unit of attention, not pixels. Linear's density feels low because chrome is muted, not because it's spacious.
cards.relevance: Reframes our 'cluttered' problem: it's high attentional density, not high pixel density. Fix is hierarchy, not whitespace.

cards.title: Philip Davis — Command Palette Interfaces
cards.key_claim: Cmd+K palettes are now table-stakes in modern SaaS (Linear / Figma / Notion / Vercel / Raycast). Best practice: fuzzy search, scoped commands, recents, keyboard-only operation.
cards.relevance: Justifies adding ⌘K palette as first-class navigation alongside the left rail.

cards.title: commandpalette.org — Command palette resources
cards.key_claim: Catalog of Cmd+K patterns across creative + dev tools; converging on identical shortcut and grouping conventions.
cards.relevance: Convention library: our palette should mirror these so users transfer muscle memory in.

cards.title: LogRocket — Tabbed navigation in UX: Where and when to use it
cards.key_claim: Tabs work for parallel siblings of equal weight; fail when content is sequential, when there are >7 tabs, or when there's strong hierarchy.
cards.relevance: Our 6 workspaces are NOT equal weight; Writing is primary, others are support. Tabs are the wrong metaphor.

cards.title: NN/G — Tabs, Used Right
cards.key_claim: Tabs work when items are mutually exclusive, parallel, and limited (~5-7). Bad tabs increase cognitive load; users miss content behind unselected tabs ~60% of the time.
cards.relevance: If we keep mode-switching at all, total mode count should stay ≤5 and modes should be co-equal.

cards.title: UserOnboard — Empty States
cards.key_claim: Empty states are first-impression real-estate; should explain the screen's purpose AND propose the first action.
cards.relevance: Our 6 workspaces almost certainly have weak empty states; opportunity to teach the product surface-by-surface.

cards.title: Smashing Magazine — The Role of Empty States in User Onboarding
cards.key_claim: Best empty states blend illustration + plain-language purpose + one primary CTA. Avoid only icon + 'No data'.
cards.relevance: Concrete pattern for empty Memory / Atlas / Simulation views.

cards.title: Ahmad Alfy — Your URL Is Your State
cards.key_claim: If your SPA forgets state on refresh, you've broken a fundamental web contract. URL is the canonical store for shareable state.
cards.relevance: Our app uses conditional rendering with no URL state — every reload bounces users to default workspace. Highest-priority fix.

cards.title: LogRocket — Advanced React state management using URL parameters
cards.key_claim: URL params + React Router + nested routes give shareable, refresh-safe workspace state with minimal code.
cards.relevance: Implementation reference: replace 1659-line conditional shell with route-driven layout.

cards.title: Wikipedia — Feature creep
cards.key_claim: Feature creep causes software bloat, schedule overruns, and reduced usability. Mitigation: separate functionality; expose extras only via opt-in submenus.
cards.relevance: Frames the 6-workspace symptom: the cure is hierarchy + opt-in, not a 7th workspace.

cards.title: UserJot — Feature Creep: Why Your Product Keeps Getting Worse
cards.key_claim: Personal/solo-built tools especially fall to feature creep because there's no PM gate. Symptom: top-level nav growth.
cards.relevance: Specific to our context: solo-built fiction app with growing top-level surface area.

cards.title: LogRocket — Overusing AI is ruining UX
cards.key_claim: Constant AI suggestions are exhausting; in creative fields users want assistive AI, not always-on AI chrome.
cards.relevance: Anti-pattern fit: Simulation/Runtime panels always-visible = AI fatigue. Move to summon-on-demand.

cards.title: UX Tigers — Hello AI Agents: Goodbye UI Design
cards.key_claim: AI in creative tools should be inline / contextual; persistent AI chrome competes with the user's own thought.
cards.relevance: Reinforces summon-on-demand for our AI features.

cards.title: Idea Theorem — AI Design Patterns for Next-Gen UX (2025)
cards.key_claim: Emerging patterns: inline copilot ribbon, side drawer, command-palette AI, ghost-text suggestions. Persistent right-rail AI is on the decline.
cards.relevance: Modern menu of options to choose from, replacing one big right-side AI panel.

cards.title: Logseq — How to work with the right-hand sidebar
cards.key_claim: Right sidebar holds stacked references / queries / linked-mentions; you can pin multiple panes vertically.
cards.relevance: Pattern for stacking 'Memory' (recent context) above 'Atlas' (entity hover) in one rail rather than separate workspaces.

cards.title: The Sweet Setup — Obsidian vs Roam vs Logseq
cards.key_claim: All three PKM tools converged on left-rail (vault/structure) + main pane (note) + right-rail (back-references/graph). Differences are stylistic, not structural.
cards.relevance: Our 'World/Memory/Atlas' merger should follow this convergent pattern.

cards.title: SAP Fiori — Content Density (Cozy and Compact) v1.96
cards.key_claim: Cozy ≈ 3rem rows / 16px base; Compact ≈ 2rem rows / 14px base. Designed for user toggle.
cards.relevance: Concrete numbers for our density toggle.
